critic_raw:
This task prompt describes the Value Alignment Verification paper but provides almost no methodological detail about the replication study itself. The critiques must therefore target the implicit methodology of reproducing this work.

The core experimental design relies on gridworld environments, which is a synthetic domain with highly controlled transition dynamics and discrete reward functions. The claim that a questionnaire can efficiently verify alignment assumes the reward function or trajectory preference space is finite and enumerable in a way that scales beyond toy domains. In gridworlds, the number of distinguishable policies is polynomial in the state space; in any continuous or high-dimensional domain, this property almost certainly breaks. The paper provides no evidence that the questionnaire size remains tractable outside gridworlds, so the efficiency claim is untested at any meaningful scale.

The framework distinguishes three settings (explicit/implicit human, explicit/implicit robot), but the replication prompt gives no indication that all three settings were independently validated. A reproduction that passes only on the explicit/explicit case (where full reward information is available) would be uninformative about the harder implicit settings, which are the novel contribution. There is no stated plan to verify which settings were actually reproduced.

For the exact alignment setting, the algorithm reduces to a combinatorial feasibility check over trajectories. The correctness guarantee depends on the assumption that the human's reward function is in the hypothesis class the algorithm searches. If the reproduction uses a different hypothesis class than the original (even by accident due to implementation detail), it could produce wrong answers silently while appearing to work on the easy test cases. No diagnostic is described to detect this.

The evaluation metric — number of questions to achieve alignment verification — is only meaningful if both the original and reproduction share the same generative process for robot policies. If the reproduction uses slightly different gridworld configurations or policy generation methods, fewer or more questions could be required without any methodological difference, making numerical comparison invalid. No protocol is described for verifying this equivalence.
all_issues_raised: The efficiency claim (minimal questionnaire size) is validated only in gridworld environments. No argument or test is provided showing the approach scales beyond discrete, finite state spaces where the number of distinguishable policies is polynomial.

The three alignment settings (explicit/explicit, explicit/implicit, implicit/implicit) have fundamentally different computational hardness. The replication gives no indication which settings were reproduced; a reproduction that only works on the easiest explicit/explicit case is insufficient to validate the contribution.

The algorithm's correctness depends on the hypothesis class for the human's reward function matching the original. Silent divergence in this implementation detail would produce plausible-looking but invalid results without any observable failure signal.

Comparing question counts between original and replication is only valid if both use the same gridworld configurations and policy generation process. No protocol for verifying this equivalence is described.
all_issues_adjudicated: The efficiency claim (minimal questionnaire size) is validated only in gridworld environments. No argument or test is provided showing the approach scales beyond discrete, finite state spaces where the number of distinguishable policies is polynomial.

The three alignment settings (explicit/explicit, explicit/implicit, implicit/implicit) have fundamentally different computational hardness. The replication gives no indication which settings were reproduced; a reproduction that only works on the easiest explicit/explicit case is insufficient to validate the contribution.

The algorithm's correctness depends on the hypothesis class for the human's reward function matching the original. Silent divergence in this implementation detail would produce plausible-looking but invalid results without any observable failure signal.

Comparing question counts between original and replication is only valid if both use the same gridworld configurations and policy generation process. No protocol for verifying this equivalence is described.
